content.text: AI Risk Taxonomy and Enterprise Risk Register

An enterprise risk register is a structured catalogue of identified risks, their likelihood and impact ratings, the controls in place to reduce them, and who owns each risk. AI systems introduce a distinct family of risks — model hallucinations, data poisoning, regulatory non-compliance with emerging AI law — that do not map cleanly onto traditional IT or operational risk categories. An AI-specific risk taxonomy extends the enterprise register with AI-native risk types, failure modes, and the controls that actually reduce them.

attrs.alt: AI risk taxonomy and heat map

content.text: A practical AI risk taxonomy for enterprise GenAI programs groups risks into five categories. Each category has distinct failure modes, owners, and control types:
— the model asserts facts not grounded in retrieved context. Highest likelihood in open-ended generation; controlled by citation-grounded prompting, confidence thresholds, and human review.
— embedding or generation quality degrades over time as production data distribution shifts away from the training distribution. Controlled by regular eval regressions against a golden test set.
— prompt injection, jailbreaks, and data poisoning that coerce the model into unintended behaviour. Controlled by input sanitisation, output guardrails, and retrieval access control.
— the LLM API (OpenAI, Anthropic, Azure) becomes unavailable or degrades silently. Controlled by fallback models, circuit breakers, and response latency monitoring.
— personal data from the retrieval corpus surfaces in model responses to users who should not have access to it. Controlled by output PII scanners and role-based retrieval access control.
— malicious content injected into the knowledge base causes the model to consistently output harmful or misleading information. Controlled by content provenance checks and ingestion-time validation.
— skewed or unrepresentative training data produces outputs that discriminate against protected groups. Controlled by bias audits and fairness evals on stratified test sets.
— the provenance chain from source document to embedding is broken, making it impossible to audit what data influenced a response. Controlled by end-to-end lineage metadata stored with each chunk.

— the data contract terms (freshness, completeness, latency) are violated, causing consumers to receive degraded data without knowing it. Controlled by automated SLA monitoring and breach notification.

— a third-party component in the AI pipeline (embedding API, vector database, orchestration library) introduces a vulnerability or breaking change. Controlled by dependency pinning, SBOM generation, and vendor SLA contracts.
— unpredicted LLM token consumption drives costs beyond budget, triggering rate limiting or service interruption. Controlled by per-user token budgets and cost alerting.
— AI system processes personal data without a valid legal basis, violates data minimisation, or cannot respond to data subject access requests within the required window.
— a system classified as high-risk under Annex III (e.g., employment decision support) lacks the required conformity assessment, technical documentation, or human oversight mechanism.
— training data or RAG corpus includes copyrighted material without a licence, and generated outputs reproduce substantial portions of that content.
— AI system outputs used as the basis for a customer-facing decision that later proves incorrect, exposing the enterprise to breach-of-contract or negligence claims.
— a high-profile AI incident (biased output, PII exposure, hallucinated advice) damages brand reputation and triggers regulatory scrutiny.
— staff distrust AI-generated content and either over-rely on it without verification, or refuse to use it, negating the investment.
— the AI system produces outputs that systematically disadvantage a protected group, creating employment law exposure and reputational harm.

The classes below model a risk register entry with inherent score (before controls) and residual score (after controls). Control effectiveness compounds with diminishing returns: two controls each 50% effective do not eliminate the risk — the second control reduces the residual 50%, giving a combined 75% reduction.

from __future__ import annotations
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional
import uuid


class RiskCategory(Enum):
    TECHNICAL    = "technical"    # model failures, hallucinations, drift, adversarial attacks
    DATA         = "data"         # poisoning, leakage, bias, lineage loss
    OPERATIONAL  = "operational"  # dependency failures, supply chain, SLA breaches
    LEGAL        = "legal"        # GDPR, EU AI Act, copyright, contractual liability
    REPUTATIONAL = "reputational" # public trust, media, employee confidence


class Likelihood(Enum):
    RARE            = 1
    UNLIKELY        = 2
    POSSIBLE        = 3
    LIKELY          = 4
    ALMOST_CERTAIN  = 5


class Impact(Enum):
    NEGLIGIBLE   = 1   # no measurable effect on business outcomes
    MINOR        = 2   # contained, easily reversed
    MODERATE     = 3   # disruption to one team or product area
    MAJOR        = 4   # cross-organisational disruption or regulatory penalty
    CATASTROPHIC = 5   # enterprise-level harm, existential threat


class RiskStatus(Enum):
    OPEN        = "open"
    MITIGATED   = "mitigated"   # controls applied, residual risk acceptable
    ACCEPTED    = "accepted"    # risk appetite decision: live with it
    TRANSFERRED = "transferred" # insurance or contractual transfer
    CLOSED      = "closed"      # risk no longer applicable


@dataclass
class RiskControl:
    description: str
    owner: str
    effectiveness: float  # 0.0 (no reduction) to 1.0 (eliminates risk)


@dataclass
class RiskEntry:
    title: str
    category: RiskCategory
    description: str
    likelihood: Likelihood
    impact: Impact
    owner: str
    controls: list[RiskControl] = field(default_factory=list)
    status: RiskStatus = RiskStatus.OPEN
    risk_id: str = field(default_factory=lambda: f"AI-{uuid.uuid4().hex[:6].upper()}")
    review_date: Optional[str] = None

    @property
    def inherent_score(self) -> int:
        """Score before any controls are applied."""
        return self.likelihood.value * self.impact.value

    @property
    def control_reduction(self) -> float:
        """Combined control effectiveness (diminishing returns)."""
        reduction = 0.0
        for ctrl in self.controls:
            reduction = reduction + ctrl.effectiveness * (1 - reduction)
        return reduction

    @property
    def residual_score(self) -> float:
        """Score after controls. Rounded to 1dp for the register."""
        return round(self.inherent_score * (1 - self.control_reduction), 1)

    @property
    def residual_rating(self) -> str:
        s = self.residual_score
        if s <= 5:   return "low"
        if s <= 9:   return "medium"
        if s <= 15:  return "high"
        return "critical"
The example below registers two AI-specific risks and shows how inherent and residual scores differ once controls are factored in:

register = RiskRegister()

# AI-001: Hallucination in customer-facing chatbot
hallucination_risk = RiskEntry(
    title="RAG hallucination — factually incorrect answers",
    category=RiskCategory.TECHNICAL,
    description=(
        "The LLM generates plausible but incorrect answers when retrieved context "
        "is insufficient or ambiguous, leading to customer misguidance on "
        "HR policies, benefits, or legal entitlements."
    ),
    likelihood=Likelihood.LIKELY,          # 4
    impact=Impact.MAJOR,                   # 4 — potential regulatory penalty
    owner="rag-platform-team",
    controls=[
        RiskControl(
            description="Citation-grounded answers: LLM instructed to only assert "
                        "claims present in retrieved chunks",
            owner="rag-platform-team",
            effectiveness=0.50,
        ),
        RiskControl(
            description="Answer confidence threshold — suppress response if "
                        "retrieval score < 0.70",
            owner="rag-platform-team",
            effectiveness=0.25,
        ),
        RiskControl(
            description="Human-in-the-loop review for high-stakes query categories",
            owner="compliance-team",
            effectiveness=0.30,
        ),
    ],
    review_date="2024-06-01",
)
register.add(hallucination_risk)

# inherent_score = 4 × 4 = 16 (critical)
# control_reduction = 0.50 + 0.25*(1-0.50) + 0.30*(1-0.625) = 0.50+0.125+0.1125 = 0.7375
# residual_score = 16 × (1 - 0.7375) = 4.2 (low!)
print(f"Inherent: {hallucination_risk.inherent_score}")
print(f"Residual: {hallucination_risk.residual_score} ({hallucination_risk.residual_rating})")

# AI-002: PII leakage via prompt injection
pii_risk = RiskEntry(
    title="PII leakage via adversarial prompt injection",
    category=RiskCategory.DATA,
    description=(
        "A malicious user crafts a prompt that coerces the LLM into revealing "
        "personal data from the retrieved context, including names, HR records, "
        "or salary information belonging to other employees."
    ),
    likelihood=Likelihood.POSSIBLE,    # 3
    impact=Impact.MAJOR,               # 4 — GDPR Article 83 fine
    owner="security-team",
    controls=[
        RiskControl(
            description="Output PII scanner (Presidio) blocks responses containing "
                        "detected PII patterns before they reach the user",
            owner="security-team",
            effectiveness=0.70,
        ),
        RiskControl(
            description="Retrieval access control — users only retrieve chunks "
                        "their role has permission to view",
            owner="iam-team",
            effectiveness=0.50,
        ),
    ],
    review_date="2024-06-01",
)
register.add(pii_risk)

print(register.summary())
# → {'total': 2, 'low': 1, 'medium': 0, 'high': 0, 'critical': 1}

The diminishing-returns formula reflects the real world: a second control rarely eliminates what the first one missed — it reduces the residual risk. Do not add controls and assume they multiply to near-zero. Measure their effectiveness empirically via red-teaming and eval suites.
Teams that use a YAML-based contract registry can express AI risks in the same format, making the risk register machine-readable for automated reporting dashboards:

# ai-risk-register.yaml — AI-specific additions to the enterprise risk register
# Each entry inherits all standard enterprise risk fields plus AI-specific extensions.

risks:
  - id: AI-001
    title: "RAG hallucination in customer-facing chatbot"
    category: technical
    sub_category: model_failure
    description: >
      LLM generates factually incorrect answers when retrieved context is
      insufficient or ambiguous. High-stakes categories: HR benefits, legal
      entitlements, medical information.
    likelihood: likely           # 4 of 5
    impact: major                # 4 of 5
    inherent_score: 16           # critical
    ai_specific:
      model: gpt-4o
      pipeline: rag
      affected_users: all_employees
      failure_mode: grounding_failure
    controls:
      - id: CTRL-001
        description: Citation-grounded prompting
        effectiveness: 0.50
        owner: rag-platform-team
      - id: CTRL-002
        description: Confidence threshold suppression (< 0.70)
        effectiveness: 0.25
        owner: rag-platform-team
    residual_score: 4.2          # low after controls
    residual_rating: low
    status: mitigated
    owner: rag-platform-team
    review_date: "2024-06-01"

  - id: AI-002
    title: "PII leakage via adversarial prompt injection"
    category: data
    sub_category: information_disclosure
    likelihood: possible         # 3 of 5
    impact: major                # 4 of 5
    inherent_score: 12           # critical boundary
    ai_specific:
      model: gpt-4o
      pipeline: rag
      regulation: GDPR_Art5_83
      failure_mode: prompt_injection
    controls:
      - id: CTRL-003
        description: Presidio PII output scanner
        effectiveness: 0.70
        owner: security-team
      - id: CTRL-004
        description: Role-based retrieval access control
        effectiveness: 0.50
        owner: iam-team
    residual_score: 2.7
    residual_rating: low
    status: mitigated
    owner: security-team

  - id: AI-003
    title: "Model drift degrades retrieval relevance silently"
    category: technical
    sub_category: model_drift
    likelihood: likely           # 4 of 5
    impact: moderate             # 3 of 5
    inherent_score: 12
    ai_specific:
      model: text-embedding-3-large
      pipeline: embedding
      failure_mode: distribution_shift
    controls:
      - id: CTRL-005
        description: Weekly embedding quality regression test vs. golden eval set
        effectiveness: 0.60
        owner: mlops-team
    residual_score: 4.8
    residual_rating: low
    status: mitigated
    owner: mlops-team

  - id: AI-004
    title: "EU AI Act compliance gap for high-risk AI system"
    category: legal
    sub_category: regulatory
    likelihood: possible         # 3 of 5
    impact: catastrophic         # 5 of 5
    inherent_score: 15
    ai_specific:
      regulation: EU_AI_Act_Art_6_Annex_III
      failure_mode: compliance_gap
    controls: []                 # remediation in progress
    residual_score: 15.0
    residual_rating: high
    status: open
    owner: legal-ai-team
    review_date: "2024-03-15"
The heat map above uses a standard 5×5 matrix. The numeric score is
Standard enterprise risk registers track likelihood, impact, controls, and owner. AI risks benefit from three additional fields:
) so the risk is scoped to a specific version. When the model changes, risks must be re-evaluated.
) that links risks across different entries and enables aggregated reporting by failure type.
: the specific regulatory clause the risk maps to (
) so compliance teams can trace from regulation to risk to control.

: at the start of an AI programme, run a structured threat-modelling session (STRIDE or MITRE ATLAS) to enumerate risks across all five categories. Populate the register before the first production deployment.
: any production incident, red-team finding, or regulatory update triggers a new or revised register entry. The risk owner is responsible for updating the entry within 5 business days.
: all open risks are re-rated. Controls that have been implemented are assessed for effectiveness. Residual scores are recalculated.
list is escalated to the AI governance board at each quarterly meeting. Any critical-rated open risk older than 90 days triggers a mandatory escalation.
AI risk taxonomy extends the enterprise register with five categories: Technical, Data, Operational, Legal/Regulatory, and Reputational/Ethical.
Risk score = likelihood × impact (both 1–5); residual score applies control effectiveness with diminishing returns.
The RiskEntry dataclass models inherent_score, control_reduction, and residual_score in a single composable structure.
AI-specific register extensions (model, failure_mode, regulation) link risks to the specific components and regulations they apply to.
Critical open risks (score 16–25 with no effective controls) require weekly review and board-level visibility.
The YAML format makes the register machine-readable for automated dashboards and compliance tooling.
